data.title: Coca-Cola FEMSA: An Undervalued Giant In Latin American Consumer Staples
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Coca-Cola FEMSA (NYSE:KOF) is the largest Coca-Cola system bottler in the world by volume. It operates in 10 countries in Latin America, with more than 2 million sales outlets and leading brands across all geographies. In Q2 2025, it faced a 5.5% YoY drop in volume due to climate and the slowdown of consumption in Mexico, but managed to keep its revenues virtually flat and preserve its operating profitability. South America, on the other hand, showed solid performance, offsetting some of that weakness.

The company is today traded with multiples well below the industry average, with an EV/EBITDA of less than 7x and a P/CF ratio close to 8x, despite having better margins, return on capital and dividend than most of its peers. Its balance sheet is solid and it has debt under control, allowing it to continue investing in growth and digitalization.

For all this, KOF today offers a rare combination from an operational point of view, its financial discipline and its attractive valuation. In this scenario, I consider it a buy, with a medium-term outlook and focus on sustainable income.

Overview

Coca-Cola FEMSA doesn’t need a presentation when we talk about beverages, but it deserves a much closer look from the financial market. Behind its name, often associated simply with bottling soft drinks, is a company that has built a robust, modern mass consumption platform deeply integrated into the daily lives of millions of people in Latin America.

From its headquarters in Mexico City, KOF coordinates a production and distribution network that reaches more than 276 million consumers in ten countries, from Mexico to Uruguay. In complex and challenging markets such as Brazil, Colombia, Argentina and the countries of Central America, its presence is not only maintained, but it grows with a level of efficiency that few companies in the sector manage to sustain. This capillarity, woven over decades, not only gives it scale but also a unique resilience within the emerging world of basic consumption.

But what distinguishes KOF today is not just its size: it is its transformation. In recent years, it has left behind the traditional role of bottler to become a smart trading platform, with digital muscle, last mile logistics and a clear customer-centric strategy.

A concrete example is Juntos+, its digital ecosystem that already has more than one million monthly active users and connects small neighborhood businesses with the entire Coca-Cola portfolio. It’s not just an app: it’s a real-time loyalty, data integration and operational execution tool designed to empower local retailers while expanding brand reach.

This process is accompanied by an ambitious investment plan: between 2024 and 2028, KOF will deploy more than 25 new production lines, expand its logistics capacity and internally reorganize its processes to gain speed, Eliminate bottlenecks and deepen a cultural transformation that is already felt throughout the organization. New principles of leadership, results orientation and disciplined execution are now part of the corporate DNA.

The relationship with The Coca-Cola Company (KO) remains strategic, but far from being a passive subordination, it is a collaborative alliance with strong local autonomy. While Coca-Cola brings global brand and vision, KOF performs with surgical precision in each market, focusing on efficiency, innovation and operational discipline.

In an environment where many investors look to developed economies for stability, Coca-Cola FEMSA offers something different: a privileged gateway to the commercial heart of Latin America, hand in hand with a global brand, an unrivalled distribution network and a process of transformation which is not yet fully recognized in its market valuation.

Q2 2025

During Q2 2025, Coca-Cola FEMSA faced a more complex context in its main markets, with adverse weather conditions in both Mexico and Brazil, and a reduction in consumption. Even so, it managed to defend its performance structure with almost stable earnings and profitability at the consolidated level.

The total volume fell 5.5% YoY, mainly affected by smaller deliveries in Mexico, Panama, Brazil and Colombia. However, consolidated revenues grew 5.0% in nominal terms to MX$72.9B, driven by good price management and favourable exchange rate effects. In comparable terms, excluding the effect of conversion to Mexican pesos, income increased by 2.4%

The operating profit remained virtually unchanged, reaching MX$9.7 million, with an operating margin of 13.4%. Adjusted EBITDA was MX$13.38B, reflecting a contraction of 3.8% YoY, in line with the more demanding operating environment and higher fixed costs.

The South America region was the strongest point of the quarter. There, revenues grew +13.2%YoY, operating profit expanded +19.6%YoY, and gross margin improved thanks to cost efficiencies and lower raw material pressure. In contrast, the division of Mexico and Central America showed weakness with a fall of 8.4% YoY in volume and 6.3% in operating profit, reflecting the adverse conditions of the Mexican market.

Net profit attributable to the controlling interest was MX$5.3B (-5.3%YoY). This result was affected by higher financial outlay, related to the issuance of bonds for $500MM with maturity in 2035, and by a lower exchange rate gain.

Valuation

The current KOF quote does not adequately reflect the operational quality and financial profile of the company in my opinion. Despite facing a more challenging environment in its major markets, KOF continues to deliver higher returns, sustained growth and strong margins, all with enviable financial discipline. However, the market values it with multiples that clearly do not do justice to this reality.

When looking at their key ratios, the discount is evident. The EV/EBITDA FWDd stands at 6.78x, 35% below the average of the basic consumption sector, while the P/E GAAP FWD is 13.8x, compared to 18.97x of the sector. Even measured against its own record, KOF trades at a discount to its five-year averages.

But beyond the picture of multiples, what is interesting is in things to come. The market consensus expects EPS growth of 31% by 2025, and although this pace will slow towards 2026 and 2027, the company maintains expansion rates that exceed most of its emerging market peers. Revenues, EBITDA and EBIT show a five-year compound growth of between 6% and 8% per annum, which speaks to a robust business model in sustained expansion.

From the point of view of profitability, KOF also stands out. Gross margin remains above 45%, ROE exceeds 17% and ROA and ROTC are well above the sector average. In addition, the adjusted net debt/EBITDA ratio is only 0.79x, which gives it enough room to continue investing in growth without compromising its balance sheet.

The dividend is also a plus point: the current return exceeds 3.8%, with a sustainable and increasing payout, which adds attractiveness for more defensive profiles. Combined with its policy of aggressive reinvestment and digital expansion, this makes KOF a rare combination of growth, profitability and yield, something difficult to find today among Latin American companies listed in New York.

In short, KOF is traded as a cyclical company when its business is clearly defensive; with stagnating multiples when its numbers show real growth. The market, perhaps affected by exposure to Mexico or a superficial reading of the quarter, seems to be ignoring the background story: KOF is an execution machine that continues to expand, and it is doing so at prices that today offer a clear opportunity for entry.

How much is Coca-Cola FEMSA worth? A realistic look at its multiples and target price

At the current price of ~$85.60 per ADR, KOF quotes a multiple EV/EBITDA FWD of approximately 6.8x, a low value if we consider the company’s operational quality, its return on capital and its sustained growth in both revenues and profits. This multiple is not only below the average of its global commodity sector peers (10.5x), but also below its own historical average of 7.3x.

To estimate how much KOF would reasonably be worth if the market recognized that intrinsic value, I developed a valuation sensitivity table. I started from a projected 2025 EBITDA of MX$54B and considered an estimated net debt of MX$45B.

For reference, each ADR represents 10 local shares, and the company has 1,680.7 MM of ordinary shares outstanding, which is equivalent to 168.07MM of ADRs. To convert assets into $, I used the updated FX of MX$18.65/$.

Thus, the results show that even without assuming increases in margins or structural improvements, a multiple of just 7.5x EV/EBITDA already implies a target price of $91.72, with a moderate upside of +7.2%.

If the market begins to reward once again the predictability of its flows, its strategic positioning in Latin America and its defensive profile, Coca-Cola FEMSA could reach levels close to $120 per ADR, representing a potential revaluation of 40%.

Risks

KOF is a solid company, but it is not immune to the ups and downs of the market. Q2 saw a drop in volumes sold, especially in Mexico, where the climate and weaker consumption hit hard. If this weakness persists or spreads to other key markets, growth could slow.

It also plays against currency exposure. When trading in countries with volatile currencies, such as Argentina or Brazil, the impact of FX can distort dollar results even when the business remains firm in local currency.

And although its valuation looks attractive, it does not guarantee an immediate revaluation. The market still punishes many Latin American stocks for simple risk aversion. In order for KOF to trade higher, it will probably first have to change the general investment climate.

Conclusions

Coca-Cola FEMSA is not a story of explosive growth, but it is a story of consistency. With solid margins, a return on capital of almost 18%, and a dominant presence in key markets in Latin America, KOF remains one of the most profitable players in the emerging consumer sector. And yet, it trades at multiples that do not reflect it: its EV/EBITDA FWD of 6.8x is closer to companies with problems than to companies with their operational profile.

Q2 showed some weakness in volumes, especially in Mexico, but the company managed to sustain revenues and profitability thanks to its scale and pricing strategy. South America has again been a driver of growth.

From these levels, I think the market seems to be ignoring the underlying quality of business. According to the valuation scenarios I developed, the reasonable upside could be between +10% and +40%, without the need for optimistic assumptions.

That is why I believe that Coca-Cola FEMSA today offers a buying opportunity for long-term investors: a stable company with sustainable dividends, well managed and with room to revalue if the market looks at the region in a different light.

data.title: My Favorite 7-10% Yielding Passive Income Machines
data.description:
I recently published an article covering some of the very best income machines that could be used to accelerate your passive income stream growth for enrichment, reward, and retirement. In particular, I discussed four of my favorite places to generate that income: infrastructure (UTF), REITs (VNQ), BDCs (BIZD), and CEFs (UTG). However, I failed to discuss my favorite place of all to invest for passive income (the midstream energy infrastructure sector (AMLP)), and a reader even commented on that article that he missed me not putting that segment in there.

With that in mind, in this article, I am going to detail my favorite place to find passive income for retirement. In particular, I will share some of my favorite 7% to 10% yielding opportunities that I have been buying hand over fist that are occupying an ever-growing majority of my portfolio.

Why I Love Energy Midstream Infrastructure

I love this sector because it enjoys enormous tailwinds right now. One of these is an increasingly favorable regulatory environment from the Trump administration, which is enabling faster approval and permitting for projects, thereby opening up additional attractive growth opportunities. Additionally, the Trump administration is pursuing an "energy dominance" agenda, which includes placing an additional emphasis on oil and natural gas production, which in turn should drive increased demand for energy infrastructure to be able to properly move that oil and gas to end markets.

Moreover, there is an explosion in energy exports, in particular to Asian and European markets, which are increasingly seeking energy security from allied countries like the United States rather than becoming dependent on potentially hostile countries like Russia, Iran, and others. As such, energy infrastructure companies are poised to benefit, as many of them are involved in the export of these energy products to these foreign markets, where demand is rapidly growing.

Last but not least, energy infrastructure is positioned to benefit immensely from the rapidly accelerating AI boom, as we are in the early innings of rapid deployment of immense sums of capital into building data centers and other essential infrastructure for the AI boom. This will all need to be powered by cheap and reliable energy, and natural gas is widely considered to be among the very best candidates for meeting this need. Therefore, strategically located natural gas infrastructure should benefit immensely from this growth in demand, thereby providing attractive growth opportunities for energy infrastructure companies. On a related note, the reshoring of American industrial capacity, especially in targeted industries, will also likely drive significant growth in energy infrastructure demand because factories and other industrial activities will need reliable and inexpensive energy as well.

When you combine all of those macro tailwinds with the fact that energy infrastructure is very stagflation resistant, given that much of it enjoys inflation-linked escalators and/or protections and has either long-duration cash flows and/or regulated agreements on the vast majority of the revenue generated by most of these companies, it is able to generate very stable cash flows through all sorts of economic environments and even potentially enjoys a bit of a tailwind when inflation is elevated. Not only that, but these companies have worked hard to improve their balance sheets in recent years and have increasingly been able to self-fund their growth projects with limited dependence on capital markets, including having well-laddered debt maturities and fairly low leverage ratios along with significant liquidity and free cash flow generation.

Putting this all together, you get a set of businesses that have durable and defensive business models, very strong balance sheets, and the ability to pay out attractive and consistently growing distributions to investors. This is particularly true in the case of MLPs in this space, as they benefit from several factors. First of all, as K1 issuers, virtually all of their distributions are classified as return of capital, which makes them very tax-efficient income machines in taxable accounts. Additionally, as pass-through entities, they prioritize paying significant distributions to investors and tend to grow those distributions at rates that match or even exceed their underlying distributable cash flow per unit growth. In the vast majority of cases, this results in consistent inflation-beating distribution increases each year, and in some cases, they even have excess cash flow left over that they then use to repurchase units opportunistically as well.

7%-Yielding Income Machine

One that I like in particular is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). EPD is the blue chip of blue chips in the midstream infrastructure space, and that is evidenced by its sector-best A- credit rating. It is run very conservatively by insiders who are well-aligned, as they own about 32% of the partnership's equity. Additionally, it has a 3.1 times leverage ratio, which is quite conservative, significant liquidity, about an 18-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, and it generates the vast majority of its cash flows from commodity-price-resistant, highly contracted sources.

Additionally, it is well-diversified across many different commodities and midstream business segments, which combine to give it some energy export exposure as well as some data center exposure through its natural gas business. Perhaps most impressively of all, it has averaged a 12% return on invested capital over the past decade while never generating below a double-digit return on invested capital, even in severe down markets for energy and/or the broader economy. As such, it has been able to grow its distribution every year for over a quarter century in a row, and today it yields about 7% on a forward-looking basis, while also being expected to grow its distribution at a mid-single-digit CAGR for years to come.

When you combine the attractive yield, the solid growth outlook, and the very conservatively run and high-quality business model, EPD is an extremely attractive source of passive income for risk-averse, income-focused investors who also care about generating a double-digit annualized total return. As such, it is one of my very largest positions as a core low-risk anchor for my portfolio.

10%-Yielding Income Machine

Meanwhile, a good bit further out on the risk spectrum lies Delek Logistics Partners (DKL), which does not have an investment-grade credit rating and is much smaller and less well-diversified than EPD is. That being said, it has some high-quality assets with significant growth potential in the Permian Basin and has a tremendous track record of growing its distribution every quarter for 48 quarters in a row and plans to continue doing so moving forward.

Meanwhile, it is making significant progress towards reducing its EBITDA exposure to its primary counterparty, Delek U.S. Holdings, and has moved from having 59% exposure in 2023 to an expected just 30% exposure to DKL this year. Additionally, it has been working to reduce its leverage ratio from 4.78 times in Q1 2023 to 4.05 times at the end of Q4 2024, while its distribution coverage ratio is expected to sit at a healthy 1.3 times this year.

All told, DKL is making a lot of progress towards shoring up its balance sheet while maintaining adequate distribution coverage, all while continuing to grow its distribution at an inflation-beating rate. Yet despite all of that, it today yields about 10%, which makes it an extremely attractive income vehicle that should not only be sustainable but should continue to grow at an inflation-beating pace for years to come, thereby delivering very attractive total returns for investors as well.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, midstream infrastructure has a lot going for it right now, both from a macro and micro level, and there are several really attractive opportunities to choose from within the sector. While I am buying several compelling opportunities in this space, EPD and DKL are two of the ones that I am particularly bullish on right now. These, along with several other opportunities, are ones that I am loading up on in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SCHG: An ETF That Can Compound With Generational Wealth Potential
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHG) is on my accumulate list for many reasons, but it has moved higher along with the rest of the market in the past 3 months, so I'm holding off on buying more for now. However, when I think about a fund that could compound over many years and possibly even build generational wealth like Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A), (BRK.B) has done for the past 60 years, I think about SCHG. I actually prefer it over Berkshire Hathaway because I believe that company does not have enough tech exposure outside of Apple Inc. (AAPL).

I believe that since we are at the very early stages of AI, humanoid robots, quantum computing, and autonomous vehicles, there will be many fortunes made in the tech sector. Because of the massive potential of these emerging technologies, I think an ETF like SCHG is a must buy for my own portfolio. This ETF is a product from The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) and it offers investors a diversified way to buy into many of the largest and most successful tech stocks and it has a low total expense ratio of just 0.040%. Let's take a closer look:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, this ETF plunged in April, along with the rest of the stock market when President Trump announced Liberation Day tariffs. However, it has experienced a powerful rebound and it is now trading above the levels it held prior to the tariff announcements. The 50-day moving average is $28.39 and the 200-day moving average is $27.22, and with this ETF trading above those levels, it appears overbought and therefore could be due for a pullback.

Top Portfolio Holdings

When I look at the top holdings for SCHG, it is like looking at a list of many of the most dominant tech companies that also have huge potential growth and upside:

Let's Review Total Returns

This ETF has put up some impressive total returns. During the past year this ETF is up about 16%, and it has achieved annual returns of around 27% over the past 3 years and around 18.7% annually over the past 5 years. Over the past ten years, it has achieved average total returns of 16.39% annually. This means that if you put $10,000 in this fund about ten years ago, it could now be valued at roughly $45,422.60. Those are impressive returns, and the kind that can build generational wealth for you and even your grandkids. While past performance is not a guarantee of future results, I would say the future looks extremely promising for many of the tech stocks that this ETF holds. Thanks to the strong total returns, this ETF has earned a 5-star rating from Morningstar.

Why This ETF Could Be A Long-Term Compounder

Many of this ETF's portfolio holdings will benefit from AI, humanoid robots, quantum computing and autonomous vehicles, and these emerging industries could be providing just what SCHG needs in order to continue to be a long-term compounder. These technologies and industries did not exist prior to our generation, so we are the first generation to have a chance at investing in them and potentially building generational wealth with them. Let's take a look at the potential for these emerging technologies:

AI:

Poised for explosive growth in the coming years and it is expected to touch every industry and individuals as well. The market for AI is expected to exceed $3.68 trillion by 2034. We are already seeing AI go mainstream in our daily lives through new search options and chatbots, but the really exciting part of AI will be when it converges with mobility and a physical presence, which will be the robots of the future.

Humanoid Robots:

AI will provide the brains for humanoid robots and in the future we could see humanoid robots everywhere, in our homes doing domestic chores, and in many businesses replacing human employees. The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) predicts that the market for humanoid robots could reach $38 billion by 2035. However, growth could go exponentially higher because Morgan Stanley (MS) sees the humanoid robot industry growing to $5 trillion by 2050. Morgan Stanley obviously sees huge opportunities for investors and it has created a "Humanoid 100" list and a number of SCHG's portfolio holdings are on the list and are expected to benefit from this major growth industry. Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) which is just over 3% of SCHG's portfolio holdings, could be a big winner in this sector if it is successful in developing its Optimus robot.

Quantum Computing:

Analysts expect the market for Quantum Computing to rise from about $1.42 billion in 2024 to around $4.24 billion in 2030. However, growth for this industry could go exponentially higher and exceed $100 billion by 2040. I really like what Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL), is doing when it comes to Quantum Computing and it has already achieved some remarkable breakthroughs. Alphabet represents over 3% of the SCHG portfolio.

Autonomous Vehicles:

Some analysts expect the market for autonomous vehicles to be worth about $134.8 billion in 2030, and that it could explode higher and be worth nearly $1 trillion by 2040. Many of the portfolio holdings of this ETF appear poised to be leaders in this market and this includes Alphabet with its Waymo division, Tesla with its Robotaxis and full self-driving vehicles and even Amazon.com, Inc.'s (AMZN) Zoox robotaxi.

Potential Risk Factors And Where I Could Be Wrong

While this ETF is diversified, it does still hold some large positions that create concentration risk. The positions it owns in NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Apple, represent just over 30% of the portfolio holdings and all of these stocks have relatively high valuations which could make them ripe for declines if growth from these companies disappoints. Also, tech stocks can be very volatile as we recently saw during the plunge in April and with the subsequent rebound. Technology is often about disruption and there is always a chance that a small company could end up disrupting one or more of the companies that this ETF has invested in.

In Summary

SCHG offers investors a way to buy into some of the largest and most promising tech companies and it does so while providing a low expense ratio and liquidity. This ETF has an impressive history of compounding strong returns over the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods. Based on this, and the significant opportunities that loom for the tech sector which includes AI, humanoid robots, quantum computing, and autonomous vehicles, I believe SCHG could continue to produce strong total returns in the coming years. For this reason, it appears to be an ideal ETF to buy and hold forever. The only caveat is that it is overbought now, so I would give it a hold rating for now and wait for pullbacks before starting a new position.

No guarantees or representations are made. Hawkinvest is not a registered investment advisor and does not provide specific investment advice. The information is for informational purposes only. You should always consult a financial advisor.

data.title: 6 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Companies that regularly raise their dividend payments are considered solid long-term investments, demonstrating financial stability. Consistent dividend growth indicates that the company is generating substantial cash flow, offering investors both income and total returns.

Out of the universe of investable companies, I track those with a history of increasing their payouts yearly. I follow the dividend increase announcements and combine them with other important data points. This compiled information is incorporated into my investment strategy, and I'm happy to share it with the Seeking Alpha community. These lists are designed to help you make informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll highlight more companies and present additional information about them, as well as the methodology behind the selection.

Here are some highlights about the group this week:

  • The average increase is 7.1%.
  • The average streak length is 19 years, with a healthy mix of newcomers and "the old guard."
  • Two offer healthy yields in the 5-7% range.
  • This week is bolstered by several financial institutions after successfully passing stress tests last month.

My investment approach centers around buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that both pay increasing dividends and can also provide better-than-average returns (determined by beating a relevant benchmark).

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining two data sources: the “U.S. Dividend Champions” spreadsheet and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This combination meshes data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company in this list has at least 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10–24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 3
Contender 1
Challenger 2

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
South Plains Financial, Inc. (SPFI) 6 1.55 28-Jul-25 6.67% Challenger
MetroCity Bankshares, Inc. (MCBS) 5 3.29 30-Jul-25 8.70% Challenger
NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN) 36 5.56 31-Jul-25 3.45% Champion
Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) 28 6.98 31-Jul-25 1.87% Champion
Morgan Stanley (MS) 12 2.81 31-Jul-25 8.11% Contender
Casey's General Stores, Inc. (CASY) 26 0.44 1-Aug-25 14.00% Champion

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
SPFI 0.15 0.16 6.67%
MCBS 0.23 0.25 8.70%
NNN 0.58 0.6 3.45%
EPD 0.54 0.55 1.87%
MS 0.93 1 8.11%
CASY 0.5 0.57 14.00%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
SPFI 41.25 29.36 42.38 8.18 40% Off Low 3% Off High
MCBS 30.43 24.24 36.15 10.01 26% Off Low 16% Off High
NNN 43.18 35.8 49.57 31.88 21% Off Low 13% Off High
EPD 31.5 27.37 34.63 10.06 15% Off Low 9% Off High
MS 142.21 90.94 145.16 10.84 57% Off Low 2% Off High
CASY 524 350.52 526.82 21.28 49% Off Low 1% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
EPD 6.98 4.4 5 3.7 3.7 10.8
NNN 5.56 2.7 3.1 2.4 3.3 8
MCBS 3.29 17.1 16.7 15.1 18.4
MS 2.81 8.8 9.7 21.5 23.5 24.4
SPFI 1.55 11.3 13.8 37.5 39
CASY 0.44 16.3 12.9 9.3 9.6 9.7

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). With a REIT included this week, I'll compare NNN against the Cohen & Steers REIT and Preferred Income Fund, Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP). RNP is great, it's a closed-end fund that has historically beaten a more common REIT fund, such as Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ), the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund.

SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $68B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

For this week's comparison, I'm including RNP on the main graph because not all companies have a 10-year dividend growth history to compare.

SCHD has returned about 197% over the past decade, and my REIT benchmark RNP has returned about 179%. Let's dig in more now:

I see two main winners in this group: Casey's General Stores and Morgan Stanley. Casey's has crushed it with a 475% return, and Morgan Stanley has been no slouch with a 370% return themselves.

After that, we hit our two indices of SCHD and RNP. For me, this is the line that any relevant dividend growth company should be able to beat. I won't invest in companies that can't match this type of performance. Unfortunately, doing these analyses, I have seen far too many that have underperformed, even in the "Champion" and "King" groups.

Investors may also have specific targets, such as yield, that they are looking for, especially in retirement. EPD is a good example of that, the total performance is fair, with 132% over the past decade, but their juicy yield is what brings a lot of investors in. So, just be aware, there are exceptions to every rule.

NNN, however, has only returned 89% over the past decade and, when compared to RNP, has significantly underperformed. RNP also has a higher yield, so that theory does not play out here either. This is why RNP has been my go-to REIT fund for some time, most individual names cannot hold a candle to it. I'm also a fan of the Neos Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI), but that's a story for a different day.

Next Steps

I tend to be quite selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. I'm on the lookout for companies that not only provide significant alpha over SCHD but also really stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what’s performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

While CASY and MS have had really solid returns, and that's my starting point for next steps, I'm just not interested in either. Each investor should have a certain amount of conviction for individual holdings, or it's hard to stick with them through tougher times. Unfortunately, convenience stores and investment banking just don't interest me, but to each their own.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ITA: Record Profitability Is Likely To Extend A Blistering Upside Momentum
data.description:
As companies across the aerospace and defense industry continue to display their substantial earnings growth power, it is still the right time to initiate or hold existing stakes in investment vehicles, such as the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (BATS:ITA). The fund's price appreciated by 35% year to date and 41% in the last twelve months. The majority of its top holdings reported a record profitability in the first half with a substantial year over year growth. In addition, they have lifted their full year forecasts. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating on ITA.

Buy and Hold ITA to Capitalize on Secular Growth Trend

After the solid performances in the past few years, iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF has been accelerating its uptrend in 2025. The market fundamentals and latest results indicate that the secular uptrend is likely to continue in the second half and ahead.

The uptrend is supported by the robust demand from both aerospace and defense segments.

Companies in the industry are beating expectations while lifting their forecasts for the full year. They are also significantly returning cash to investors through dividends and share buybacks.

For example, in the June quarter, GE Aerospace (GE), ITA's top holding with a portfolio weight of 20%, generated 24% and 64% year over year revenue and profit growth. The company's financial performance was driven by a 30% revenue increase in its commercial engines and services segment.

Given the strong growth in orders backlog, GE now expects full year adjusted revenue to increase in a mid teen percentage compared to the previous forecast of a low double digit percentage growth. The earnings per share is also expected to increase in the range of $5.60-$5.80 compared to the previous forecast of $5.10-$5.45.

Furthermore, the company also lifted its long term profitability and cash flow outlook, now expecting to achieve $11.5 billion in operating profit and $8.5 billion in free cash flow in 2028. This is up by $1.5 billion from its recent outlook. GE's share price rallied 57% in the last twelve months as its exceptional financial performance and outlook continued to boost investors confidence.

RTX Corp (RTX), ITA's second largest holdings with a portfolio weight of 13%, reported second quarter adjusted revenue growth of 9% to $21.6 billion. Its segment operating profit increased by 12% and adjusted earnings per share grew by 11% from the previous year.

RTX also lifted its full year top-line outlook, supported by 15% year over year and 9% sequential order backlog growth. It now expects its full year revenue in the range of $84.75 billion to $85.5 billion. The company also hiked its quarterly dividend by 8%.

Over the past few months, Boeing's (BA) share price regained upside momentum, fuelled by a strong demand for its commercial aerospace segment. Its commercial segment reported a massive year over year revenue growth of 89% in the first quarter of 2025. The defense segment's revenue growth was 16% year over year. Wall Street expects 27% revenue growth for Boeing in fiscal 2025. Its shares are up 30% year to date.

TransDigm Group (TDG), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), General Dynamics (GD), L3Harris Technologies (LHX), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Axon Enterprise (AXON) are among its top 10 holdings. ITA's top 10 holdings make up 71% of its entire portfolio.

Like the top 3 holdings, TransDigm, Howmet Aerospace and General Dynamics saw robust share price gains so far in 2025. TransDigm Group is expected to generate a low double digit earnings increase in 2025 while Howmet Aerospace could experience 30% earnings growth.

Whereas, General Dynamics Corp reported year over year revenue growth of 8.9% in the second quarter of 2025. Its earnings per share jumped 14% to $3.74 per share. The company’s backlog surged to a record $103.7 billion, with the new orders accounting for $28.3 billion. Phebe Novakovic, GD's chair and chief executive, seems optimistic about the company's performance. Here is what he stated:

"During the first half of the year, each of our four segments achieved growth in revenue and earnings, with margins on a companywide basis expanding 50 basis points over the same period last year. Our strong cash flow and healthy backlog position us well to have a good second half."

Besides Lockheed Martin (LMT), its rest of the top 10 holdings are delivering solid returns and financial growth.

Overall, the defense side is benefiting from geopolitical tensions and record defense spending from major countries. Countries across the globe are seeking to equip their armies with the latest equipment and technologies.

On the other hand, the aerospace side is witnessing blistering growth amid the increased demand for commercial aircraft and other services. The global commercial aircraft order backlog hit a new high in May, surpassing 16,000 aircraft for the first time.

Quant Rating and Other Characteristics

ITA received a buy rating with a high quant score of 4.41. A buy rating based on the data-driven analysis also vindicates my fundamental thesis.

ITA's robust price momentum and strong liquidity helped it achieve a high quant score. Its expense ratio of 0.40% is lower than the median of all ETFs. The consecutive years of dividend payments and growth also makes it an interesting option for the long term.

Although the fund is trading at expensive valuations given its trailing price to earnings ratio of 38 and price to book ratio of 5.69, I believe lofty valuations may not dent its performance. This is because the market reward companies and ETFs with the greater growth prospects. In addition, the strong forward earnings growth forecasts from aerospace & defense companies are likely to support the valuations in the following quarters.

Risk Factors

The aerospace & defense industry is exposed to various risk factors. For example, the industry's performance depends on global geopolitical conditions. In addition, economic challenges can also lower the industry's revenue and earnings growth power. In the case of ITA, the fund's standard deviation is higher than the median of all ETFs. In addition, its annualized volatility of 22% is greater than 16% of the median of all ETFs.

Final Thoughts

As upside momentum for the aerospace & defense industry is well-established with expectations for further acceleration in the coming quarters, it is still the right time to initiate a position. Investment options, such as iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF, could be the right vehicle to capitalize on the uptrend. The fund offers access to a portfolio of 38 aerospace & defense companies. Its price momentum is strong, the expense ratio is low, and liquidity is high.

data.title: Templeton Global Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Tariffs dominated much of the news cycle during 2025’s second quarter, which began with US President Donald Trump denoting April 2 as “Liberation Day” and announcing wide-ranging tariff measures. Negotiations around final tariff levels for a number of countries were still taking place by the end of the quarter. The US dollar (USD) continued to weaken during the period. Actual inflation outcomes have largely been benign, but there has been some halt in progress, and higher inflation expectations (related to tariffs) engender caution about future paths. Global monetary policy remains in an easing cycle, though officials have voiced concern about the effects of tariffs on growth and inflation. The US Federal Reserve kept the federal funds target rate unchanged at 4.25%–4.50% during the quarter. Geopolitical conflicts flared up again during the period, including a brief conflict between India and Pakistan and a slightly longer one between Israel and Iran, though there has been no lasting impact on financial and commodity markets.
  • Contributors: Currency positions contributed to absolute fund performance for the quarter, as did interest-rate strategies and sovereign credit exposures.
  • Detractors: The fund’s net-negative position in the euro detracted from absolute results.
  • Outlook: We continue to closely monitor the evolution of tariffs and their potential impacts on US and global growth, inflation and monetary policy across countries.

Performance Review

  • Currencies: The USD weakened further during the quarter, with most currencies stronger against it. Positions in the Mexican peso, Brazilian real, Japanese yen, South Korean won, Malaysian ringgit, Ghanaian cedi, Australian dollar and Serbian dinar contributed to absolute performance, while the fund’s net-negative position in the euro detracted. Despite its pullback this year, we believe the USD remains at stretched valuation levels against a number of currencies.
  • Interest-Rate Strategies: Most developed market sovereign bond yields fell over the period, as did those in most emerging markets. Duration exposures in Brazil, South Africa and Australia contributed to absolute fund results. We aim at a relatively high overall portfolio yield by holding higher-yielding local-currency positions in specific emerging markets.
  • Credit: Emerging market debt indexes generally were stronger over the quarter. USD-denominated sovereign credits contributed to absolute fund return.
  • Positioning: We continue to favor select countries in both developed and emerging markets where we see value in their currencies, bonds or both. Our portfolio is constructed to capture potential upside in currencies we consider to be undervalued against the USD, and which also have robust balances of payments and growth fundamentals. In bonds, we hold positions in select countries where we identified opportunities from various factors, including inflation and interest-rate outlooks, fiscal progress and other economic reforms, and/or reshoring. Taking all of these factors into account, our top local- currency exposures include Japan, Brazil, Australia, Mexico, India, Malaysia and South Korea. We hold a net-negative position against the Chinese yuan, as we expect various factors (including but not limited to tariffs) to undermine the currency over the medium term. We expect this position to also help hedge our portfolios against potential risks to other emerging market currencies. In hard-currency-denominated sovereign credit positions, we favor emerging markets with improving fundamentals and policy responses. In all positions, we remain highly selective at the sovereign level, given significant variations in economic fundamentals and policy responses.

Outlook

  • We continue to monitor the evolution of tariffs to assess their ultimate impact on US and global growth. Regardless of where tariffs eventually settle, it is likely growth will be negatively affected as the prevailing uncertainty is likely to be detrimental to investment spending, as well as some types of consumer spending. Inflation risks are higher too. While global inflation remains quite contained to date, shorter-term setbacks in some countries may become more permanent if tariff-related increases become embedded in inflation. The current heightened uncertainty also adds to forecast risk around global monetary policy. Many countries are still somewhere in an easing cycle, with some relatively late starters and others near the end of their cycles. One main exception is the Bank of Japan normalizing policy in response to structural reflationary conditions.
  • Even with the depreciation so far this year, the USD remains broadly overvalued, in our assessment. While some currencies are still vulnerable against the USD, we expect a number to gain against the greenback. This view factors in cyclical factors and structural ones, as well as a pivot in financial asset flows away from the United States as investors react to the new US administration’s policies. Consequent to this view, we see opportunity in non-USD assets in select countries within both developed and emerging markets.
  • Geopolitics and politics: We continue to closely monitor developments, including the latest developments in the Middle East. Upper House elections in Japan are due to be held on July 20.

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark =FTSE World Government Bond Index

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 1/2/1997. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 1/2/1997, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (B) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 04/30/2026 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls.

Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Active and frequent trading may increase a shareholder’s tax liability and transaction costs. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Reflation is a fiscal or monetary policy designed to expand a country’s output and curb the effects of deflation.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The FTSE World Government Bond Index (“WGBI”) measures the performance of fixed-rate, local-currency, investment-grade sovereign bonds. The WGBI currently comprises sovereign debt from multiple countries, denominated in a variety of currencies. The WGBI provides a broad benchmark for the global sovereign fixed income market. Source: FTSE.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Efiective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Credit Quality is a measure of a bond issuer’s ability to repay interest and principal in a timely manner. The credit ratings shown are based on each portfolio security’s rating as provided by Standard and Poor’s, Moody’s Investors Service and/or Fitch Ratings, Ltd. and typically range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest), or an equivalent and/or similar rating. For this purpose, the manager assigns each security the middle rating from these three agencies. When only two agencies provide ratings, the lower of the two ratings will be assigned. When only one agency assigns a rating, that rating will be used. Foreign government bonds without a specific rating are assigned a country rating, if available. Securities that are unrated by all three agencies are reflected as such. The credit quality of the investments in the Fund’s portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the Fund. These ratings are updated monthly and may change over time. Please note, the Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 3 Reasons To Buy PDD Holdings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last wrote about the Chinese retailer and e-commerce app Temu owner, PDD Holdings (NASDAQ:PDD) over a year ago, its price has underwhelmed. It's down by over 12% since. But here's the rub. Zoning into the stock's performance in 2025 alone reveals a different picture, with double-digit gains seen despite ongoing macroeconomic concerns (see chart below).

Among peers, its performance is second only to Alibaba (BABA), with JD.com (JD) seeing a contraction at this time. It has also surpassed the market indices Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP) and S&P 500 (SP500) YTD, which is a point to note considering that PDD's performance is starkly weaker compared to February 2024-present by comparison with both indices seeing a double-digit increase over this time compared with PDD's pullback.

I believe there's merit to the current rise and that there's now an opportunity to buy the stock while it's still at relative lows. Here are three reasons why.

3 Positives for PDD

When I last wrote, there weren't any catalysts for a price increase. For starters, its higher market multiples compared to peers. Then the relatively muted recovery in China's consumer market as well as the need for bigger marketing spends to boost the international market weren't encouraging either.

While challenges definitely persist, there are positives to note as well, which gain particular significance at a time that the stock's metrics have improved. These are as follows.

#1. China's Retail Sales Growth Accelerates

While China's retail sales still aren't growing at their pre-pandemic rates even now, recent acceleration is hard to miss, indicating that the Chinese government's efforts to revitalize the economy with multiple policy stimuli are working. Average monthly growth has been 5.4% YoY in Q2 2025, which doubles the increase of 2.7% YoY in the corresponding quarter of last year.

Online retail sales growth of 8.5% YoY in the first half of 2025 is even more encouraging since it's higher than the 5% YoY increase seen for overall retail sales of consumer goods during this time. This bodes well for PDD, whose revenue growth wasn't bad in Q1 2025 at 10% YoY, but it was certainly a massive come off from the 59% increase seen for the full year 2024.

While it's most unlikely that the recent retail sales pickup in PDD's home country would bring revenue growth back up to its 2024 levels, it can help. If nothing else, it can balance the negative impact the company is facing in the U.S. market with tariff increases.

I'd go so far as to say that I won't rule out the possibility of an upgrade from the current full-year revenue projection of 9.9% YoY by analysts for 2025. It's worth pointing out here that at the top end, analysts still see the possibility of an 18.5% increase this year.

#2. Profit Contraction for a Purpose

Besides the slowing down in revenue growth, a big disappointment in the latest earnings release was the sharp drop in profits. After an 80% increase in both non-GAAP operating profit and non-GAAP net profit in 2024, the figures fell by 36% YoY and 45% YoY, respectively, in Q1 2025.

While the natural assumption here would be that a slowing down in demand has affected earnings, it turns out that there's a strategic reason for the profit drop. PDD increased investments in the platform to cope with "changes in the external environment," as the company puts it.

With these investments, it aims to provide support to both merchants and consumers in order to raise sales and lower costs. PDD is committed to further investments going forward, indicating that there's likely to be a continued impact on profits.

It's not a sign to be diffident about PDD, though, as the three points suggest:

  • First, analysts' estimates still put the expected EPS drop at 25.9% this year, which is a far smaller contraction than that seen in Q1 2025.
  • Second, the company hopes that bigger expenses on investments will improve its long-term health.
  • Third, in any case, from next year onwards, it's expected to go back to clocking double-digit earnings growth (see graphic below).

#3. Market Multiples Are More Attractive Now

Finally, the stock's market multiples are far more attractive now than they were when I last checked. Here's how:

  • PDD's forward non-GAAP ratio at 13.56x is far lower than its five-year average of 71.8x. But even if the current uncertain environment doesn't allow for bullishness on the stock, the ratio isn't terribly different from Alibaba (BABA) and JD.com (JD), which are at 13.64x and 11.84x, respectively.
  • The forward GAAP P/E at 14.95x is far more muted than the 24.85x level it was in last February.
  • At that time, PDD was also trading far ahead of BABA and JD. While BABA was at half its level, JD was a just sub-10x ratio. Now, however, it compares favorably with BABA at 16.05x and with JD at 13.39x; the gap has narrowed considerably here as well.

What Can Go Wrong

With the current environment of uncertainty, the discussion would be incomplete with a consideration of risks to the stock. The most glaring of which is the company's international markets. While the U.S. and China have reached some agreements, given the long-standing friction between the two countries, there's always the possibility of disruptions again. This can impact PDD's fortunes going forward.

Next, attempts at reviving the Chinese economy have been going on for a while, but the results are limited so far. The state of the consumer market still poses concerns, with consumer price inflation almost flat. If the recent revival in retail sales turns out to be a flash in the pan, PDD would be affected by challenges in both the domestic and international markets.

What Next?

Still, the drop in the stock's market multiples, especially compared to past levels, is notable and indicates that there's still a case to buy PDD while it's still trading at lows. Some signs of revival in China are evident, and from next year onward, PDD's profits are expected to be back in good health again. Given the potential shown by the company and the stock in the past, on balance, the current tough times are an opportunity to buy the stock. I'm upgrading it to Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JETS: Seasonal Headwinds In Play, Muted 'Big 4' YTD Performances
data.description:
The major airline stocks have generally struggled in 2025. Shares of American Airlines (AAL) are down the most, more than 33% YTD, while Southwest Airlines (LUV) slipped into the red for the year following its soft Q2 earnings report. The other two major components of the U.S. Global Jets ETF (NYSEARCA:JETS), United Airlines (UAL), and Delta Air Lines (DAL) are down between 5% and 10% in 2025.

Despite the tepid equity performances, the latest TSA checkpoint numbers are impressive. The tally tops year-ago levels, which were already record-breaking. Amid this hazy backdrop, I reiterate a hold rating on JETS. Shares are up 16% since my neutral stance in October of 2024, but it has been a bumpy ride, and I see potential resistance on the chart. I will also update the valuation assessment today.

TSA Checkpoint Number Climb Above Year-Ago Levels

According to the issuer, JETS provides investors access to the global airline industry, including airline operators and manufacturers from all over the world. It invests in stocks of companies operating across industrials, capital goods, aerospace and defense, passenger and cargo aircraft and airplanes, commercial passenger airplanes, transportation, air freight and logistics, passenger airlines, transportation infrastructure, airport services, airports, and airport terminal services sectors.

JETS is a small ETF, now with assets under management of just $906 million, which is down from $1.1 billion nine months ago. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 60 basis points, while there is no trailing 12-month dividend yield.

Share price momentum has been strong lately, earning the product a solid A ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system. Of course, being a concentrated industry ETF with direct ties to a very cyclical slice of the global economy, its historical standard deviation metrics are elevated, resulting in a poor risk rating.

Liquidity is high, however. Average daily volume is north of three million shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just a single penny at the time of my writing. Importantly, the four top holdings have all reported Q2 results, so we may see lighter volatility in the weeks ahead.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 1-star ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box, but there is a clear lean towards value over growth. Moreover, just 20% of JETS is classified as large cap, so the high weight toward global SMID caps adds to its macroeconomic exposure.

I'd also call out that a material 24% of the allocation is invested in ex-U.S. equities. Some international access may be beneficial if we see the US Dollar Index continue to struggle. As for the valuation, JETS consistently trades at a discount to the S&P 500's P/E, and we see that today with its low 11.4x price-to-earnings ratio.

JETS: Holdings & Dividend Information

Sector-wise, Industrials is the heavy hitter, as you'd expect. Almost 90% of the ETF is in that area, which is the top-performing of the 11 S&P 500 groups.

While 11% of the fund is comprised of Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology names, most shares move in tandem based on the latest travel trends and bookings data.

JETS: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, the August to September stretch has historically been soft for JETS, so this augurs for a measured stance. November has been an unusually bullish month over the past 10 years, so patience may be prudent over the months ahead.

JETS: Bearish August-September Trends

The Technical Take

With a low valuation but high cycle risks and lackluster seasonal trends on tap, JETS' technical situation remains questionable. Notice in the chart below that resistance is still in play from several years ago. The $27 to $29 range has been a selling spot on a pair of occasions after the ETF broke down in 2020. To its credit, JETS took out near-term resistance that I highlighted last time, in the low-mid $20s. I do like that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is in a bullish zone.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average—it's on the rise, indicating that the bulls control the primary trend. We also have a bullish golden cross pattern that just occurred in which the 50 dma moved above the 200 dma. Moreover, there's now a high amount of volume by price below today's level. Buying on a pullback to the $22–23 range could be the ideal play as the third quarter progresses.

JETS: High $20s Resistance, Rising 200 dma, and Strong RSI Are Pluses

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on JETS. The valuation is decent, but I see the fund near key long-term resistance, while YTD performance among its largest positions has not been impressive.
